Trump subsequently denied colluding with the Department of Justice to file criminal charges against Federal Reserve Chairman Powell, stating that the federal court's subpoena for Powell was unrelated to whether the Fed would cut interest rates. However, he continued to pressure Powell and the Fed on interest rates. Trump stated that Powell was either incompetent or dishonest, and that he was not doing a good job. As tensions between Trump and Powell escalated, market sentiment was disturbed, with Bitcoin continuing to rise, while US stock index futures and the dollar fell. However, the market does not believe this dispute will escalate to the point of leading to Powell's early departure. IV. Trump Wants to Regulate Credit Lending, Setting an "Cap" on Interest Rates Following the market turmoil in the mortgage sector, US President Trump has again targeted the consumer credit market, calling for stricter controls on credit card interest rates. Last Friday, Trump again tweeted on Truth Social, calling for a 10% cap on credit card interest rates for one year, starting January 20, 2026. He claimed this move was intended to prevent the American public from continuing to be "extorted" by credit card companies. While this proposal echoes a promise he made during his 2024 campaign, the statement provided no specific details on how the plan would be implemented or how to enforce corporate compliance. Market analysts generally believe that without congressional support, a mere executive call may be difficult to enforce legally. Hedge fund manager Bill Ackman publicly opposed the move, warning that if interest rates are forcibly lowered, causing lenders to be unable to cover potential losses and achieve reasonable returns on equity, banks will have to massively cancel credit card services for consumers. He further elaborated on the potential ripple effects, suggesting that millions of consumers shut out of the formal financial system may ultimately have to resort to informal lending channels, incurring higher interest rates and worse terms. V. US December Core CPI Cools Down Last night, the US December core consumer price index (CPI) was released, showing a year-on-year increase of 2.7%, in line with expectations and unchanged from the previous month. Following the data release, traders increased their bets, believing the Federal Reserve might not wait until Fed Chairman Powell's term ends in May before cutting interest rates, as the Bureau of Labor Statistics previously reported that basic consumer price increases were slightly lower than expected. Although traders still believe a June rate cut is the most likely outcome, the latest data predicts a 42% probability of a Fed rate cut in April, up from 38% before the data release. Nick Timiraos, a Fed mouthpiece, stated that the December CPI data is unlikely to change the Fed's current wait-and-see attitude, as officials are likely to want to see more evidence that inflation is stabilizing and gradually declining before cutting rates. To resume interest rate cuts, Federal Reserve officials may need to see new evidence that labor market conditions are deteriorating or that price pressures are easing. The latter may require at least several more months of inflation data to become apparent. Following the release of CPI data, the three major US stock indices closed lower, US Treasury bonds traded sideways, the dollar strengthened and recovered yesterday's losses, and the commodity and cryptocurrency markets performed strongly. VI. Market Analysis and Interpretation Based on recent macroeconomic developments and major financial market trends, the cryptocurrency market has rebounded significantly. What will the market trend be next, and how should we interpret it? Let's look at the main market analyses. 1. Bitwise Chief Information Officer Matt Hougan stated in an article on the X platform that if ETF demand continues in the long term, the price of BTC will enter a parabolic growth phase. Matt Hougan cited the example of a 65% increase in gold prices in 2025, pointing out that the prices of both gold and BTC are determined by supply and demand. Following the US seizure of Russian government bonds in 2022, central banks' annual gold purchases increased from approximately 500 tons to about 1,000 tons and remained stable thereafter. This demand altered the supply-demand balance, but this wasn't immediately reflected in prices. Gold prices rose 2% in 2022, 13% in 2023, and 27% in 2024, only experiencing a parabolic increase in 2025. This was because the demand in previous years was met by holders willing to sell gold; when selling pressure subsided and demand persisted, prices surged. A similar situation exists with BTC and ETFs. Since the ETFs' debut in January 2024, their purchases have exceeded 100% of the new BTC supply. Because existing holders are willing to sell, prices haven't yet entered a parabolic phase. If ETF demand continues, the selling pressure from existing sellers will eventually be exhausted.

2. VanEck stated that entering 2026, fiscal and monetary policy signals are becoming increasingly clear, and the market as a whole is more risk-averse. Artificial intelligence, private lending, gold, and crypto assets, after adjustments, are showing more attractive investment opportunities. The market environment in 2026 presents a scene investors haven't seen in years: a clear market outlook. Clear expectations surrounding fiscal and monetary policy directions and core investment themes support a more constructive, risk-averse strategy, although a high degree of discernment is still needed in asset selection. Regarding the crypto market, Bitcoin's traditional four-year cycle was broken in 2025, making short-term signals more complex. This divergence makes a cautious assessment of the trend over the next 3–6 months more likely. However, this view is not an internal consensus; disagreements remain within the team, with Matthew Sigel and David Schassler holding a relatively more positive view on the short-term performance of the current cycle. 3. Glassnode's report points out that Bitcoin's implied volatility has fallen back into the low-expectation range, indicating that the market's expectations for significant volatility in the next quarter are relatively mild, and it will return to a low-volatility state. This reflects limited short-term hedging demand and usually foreshadows a faster price readjustment once market volatility re-emerges, as positions are adjusted based on new information. Furthermore, profit-taking by long-term Bitcoin holders has cooled to levels typically observed in the early stages of a bear market. This situation is usually associated with high uncertainty and often occurs during stagnation in the middle of a bull market or in the early stages of a deeper bear market. 4. Standard Chartered Bank sends a positive signal on Ethereum's prospects. Geoffrey Kendrick, Global Head of Digital Asset Research at Standard Chartered, stated that although he lowered his recent price forecast against the backdrop of overall crypto market weakness, he expects Ethereum to outperform other cryptocurrencies in 2026. The report points out that Bitcoin's continued dominance in the sector and its underperformance have weakened the appreciation prospects of digital assets relative to the US dollar. Standard Chartered Bank expects the ETH/BTC exchange rate to gradually return to the high level of around 0.08 seen in 2021, thanks to Ethereum's structural advantages that other cryptocurrencies lack, including its dominance in stablecoins, real-world assets, and decentralized finance, as well as ongoing network scaling efforts. The bank now expects Ethereum to reach $7,500 by the end of 2026, a downward revision from its previous forecast of $12,000; the 2027 and 2028 targets have been adjusted to $15,000 and $22,000 respectively, both lower than earlier expectations. However, Standard Chartered Bank has raised its long-term outlook, increasing its forecast for the end of 2029 to $30,000 and adding a target of $40,000 by the end of 2030. 5. Crypto analyst Willy Woo wrote an article expressing optimism about Bitcoin's performance from late January to February. Willy Woo stated, "Our internal investor fund flow model predicted that Bitcoin bottomed out on December 24th and has been steadily strengthening since then. Typically, it takes 2-3 weeks for this to be reflected in the price, and this is arguably happening now (though suppressed by short-term overbought technical indicators). Another positive factor is that paper currency liquidity (futures market) is recovering after months of stagnation, similar to mid-2021, which facilitated the second top of the previous cycle. Therefore, the $98,000-$100,000 resistance level needs to hold. If this resistance is broken, the ATH resistance level will need to be observed next."

6. On-chain data analyst Murphy wrote that the on-chain indicator RMMPC shows that Asian market funds are currently more dominant in influencing BTC prices, with strong intervention starting after December 19, 2025, simultaneously pushing BTC to a second pullback to $8.5.

After reaching tens of thousands of dollars, a rebound began. However, current market funds in the Americas have not yet actively entered the market (partly due to the Christmas and New Year holidays), resulting in limited rebound strength. Previously, before the market rally in March 2025, Asian funds entered first, followed by US funds, ultimately providing strong momentum and changing the market direction. The RMMPC indicator, short for Regional Month-over-Month Price Change, is mainly used to observe the dominance and driving force of investor sentiment and fund flows in different regions on Bitcoin prices.
